turns.content: I'm trying to build a startup in Rust using Axum, but I keep getting killed by SIGTERM without finishing my requests. How do I stop the server from just exiting immediately when it receives a signal?

That happens because the default behavior is to exit as soon as the OS sends a termination signal. To handle this properly, you need to listen for signals like SIGINT or SIGTERM using tokio::signal and then call shutdown on your server instance before exiting.
So I should use something like tokio::signal::ctrl_c? And once that fires, what exactly do I pass into the shutdown function?

Yes, you can wrap the signal handler in a tokio::select! block to await the termination event while running your server loop. Once the signal arrives, you should call axum::Server::with_graceful_shutdown on your Server instance, which will start draining in-flight requests before actually exiting the process.
Wait, I thought with_graceful_shutdown just stopped accepting new connections? Does it still handle existing ones?

That's a great point of confusion. With graceful shutdown enabled, the server doesn't just drop connections; it actively drains in-flight requests by processing them to completion before shutting down. This ensures your API returns complete responses instead of cutting users off mid-request.
I see, so I need to make sure my async runtime handles the signal correctly before calling shutdown. But what if someone is already sending a request when the signal arrives?
If a request arrives after the shutdown trigger but before the server actually exits, the graceful mechanism will still process it fully since it waits for the current task to complete.

I'm building an automation script in Rust and want to handle shutdown events properly. I saw examples using tokio::signal::ctrl_c but I'm confused about how to integrate that with axum so the server doesn't just crash when I press Ctrl+C.

That's a common point of confusion. You need to use axum::Server::with_graceful_shutdown which accepts a closure that returns a Future. This allows you to run your shutdown logic concurrently with the server listening for requests, ensuring no in-flight requests are abruptly killed.
Okay so I should bind ctrl_c signals to the Server? But what exactly does that closure receive and how do I drain pending connections?

The closure receives an axum::Server<Router> instance. Inside your shutdown logic, you typically spawn a task that calls server.stop(). This triggers the graceful draining of in-flight requests before the process exits.
Got it, but I'm still worried about race conditions. What if new requests come in while my shutdown logic is running? Does axum guarantee they won't get processed?

Yes, when you call server.stop() on the Server object, it immediately stops accepting new connections. Any request already in flight will be allowed to complete until their response is fully written before being dropped.
That makes sense. But I'm seeing some code using tokio::spawn for the shutdown task and others calling it directly on the server thread. Which approach is more idiomatic?

The most idiomatic pattern wraps the Server in a graceful_shutdown() method that returns a (Server, ShutdownHandle). You spawn a tokio::spawn task to handle the signal and pass the shutdown handle into your main loop. This keeps the control flow clean.
Ah so I shouldn't be manually managing signals in my main function? And what happens if I forget to run this setup?
If you don't configure graceful shutdown, the server will just terminate immediately when the process exits. No draining happens. You'll lose any ongoing requests which is bad for reliability.
